forecasts.reasoning: John Morgan explicitly stated in mid-May 2025 that he intends to wait until September 2026 (after the major party primaries) to decide on and announce a run for Florida governor. While he faces an impending June 2025 statutory deadline to register a new political party and/or switch his affiliation to comply with Florida's 365-day rule before the June 2026 qualifying period, doing so does not require him to formally announce a gubernatorial campaign. Morgan is a self-funded billionaire who explicitly noted he does not need to open a campaign account early. Given his strong, repeated insistence on waiting to see the political landscape develop before entering the race, the probability that he will formally announce his candidacy before July 1, 2025, is extremely low.

The Bulgargaz-BOTAS agreement has become a severe financial burden for Bulgaria, leaving Bulgargaz on the verge of bankruptcy with massive arrears. While there is immense political pressure in Bulgaria to exit the 13-year deal (evidenced by Borissov's recent appeal to Erdogan), terminating the contract triggers a devastating penalty of around BGN 4 billion (approx. $2.2 billion). Bulgarian officials, including the Energy Minister, have repeatedly acknowledged this legal trap, reducing the likelihood of a voluntary unilateral cancellation by Bulgaria. On the other hand, Turkey has consistently shown no willingness to void the lucrative deal. While a unilateral announcement or a default leading BOTAS to formalize termination remains a possibility, the sheer scale of the financial and diplomatic fallout encourages both sides to drag out negotiations rather than force an abrupt rupture. Given the extremely tight window (less than 25 days until July 1, 2025), the probability of an official cancellation announcement occurring in this short timeframe is low.

Sudan has been embroiled in a severe, devastating civil war since April 2023, severely compromising state capacity and legislative functions. As of April 2025, Sudan was explicitly listed as having signed but not yet ratified the AfCFTA agreement, being one of only a handful of remaining non-ratifying African countries. Given that the forecast date is June 6, 2025—leaving less than a month until the July 1, 2025 resolution deadline—and there are no reports of any imminent legislative action by the Sudanese government to ratify the agreement amid the existential conflict, the likelihood of ratification occurring in the next three weeks is extraordinarily low.
